docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets
docs.content: The financial world offers many ways to participate in price movements and global trends. Four of the most popular trading arenas for retail traders are: Forex (currencies) Metals (like gold and silver) Crypto (digital assets) Commodities (such as oil, gas, and agricultural products) Each market has its own behavior, drivers, and risks. This guide gives you a clear starting point so you can understand where each market might fit into your trading journey.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Why Traders Explore Multiple Markets
docs.content: New traders often start with one market but quickly realize that diversification matters. Different markets react to different events: Currencies react to interest rates and economic data Metals respond to risk sentiment and inflation expectations Crypto is heavily driven by technology adoption and market sentiment Commodities move with supply–demand, geopolitics, and weather By understanding the core characteristics of each, you can decide which market aligns best with your goals, time, and risk tolerance.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: 1. Forex: Trading Global Currencies
docs.content: Forex, short for foreign exchange, is the market where one currency is exchanged for another. It is the largest financial market in the world, with daily volumes in the trillions of dollars.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: How Forex Trading Works
docs.content: In forex, you trade currency pairs, such as: EUR/USD – Euro vs US dollar GBP/JPY – British pound vs Japanese yen When you buy a pair, you are buying the base currency and selling the quote currency. If EUR/USD goes up, it means the euro has strengthened relative to the dollar.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Key Features of Forex
docs.content: 24/5 market across major financial centers High liquidity, especially in major pairs Often tight spreads on popular instruments Ability to trade with leverage (which also increases risk)

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Typical Forex Terms
docs.content: Pip: Smallest unit of movement in a currency pair (usually 0.0001). Lot: Standard trade size (standard, mini, micro). Spread: Difference between bid (sell) and ask (buy) prices. Leverage: Controls a larger position with smaller capital—use carefully.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: 2. Metals: Gold, Silver, and Safe-Haven Assets
docs.content: Precious metals such as gold (XAU) and silver (XAG) have long been considered stores of value. In modern trading, you can speculate on metal prices without physically holding them, often via CFDs.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Why Traders Look at Metals
docs.content: Safe-haven appeal: Gold often draws attention during uncertainty, market stress, or high inflation. Diversification: Metals behave differently from stocks or currencies, making them a useful diversifier. Clear macro drivers: Prices are influenced by interest rates, inflation data, central bank policy, and geopolitical events.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Common Traded Metals
docs.content: Gold (XAU/USD): Often viewed as a hedge against inflation and currency weakness. Silver (XAG/USD): Has both industrial use and store-of-value characteristics.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Things to Keep in Mind
docs.content: Metals can be volatile during major news events. They are often traded with leverage, which can magnify both profits and losses. Market sentiment can shift quickly during risk-on or risk-off moves.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: 3. Crypto: Digital Assets and Volatility
docs.content: Crypto markets focus on digital assets such as Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH). These assets trade on blockchain-based networks and are known for their high volatility and 24/7 trading.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: What Makes Crypto Different
docs.content: 24/7 market: Unlike forex or traditional commodities, crypto does not close on weekends. High volatility: Large percentage moves in a short time are common, which can create both opportunity and risk. Narrative-driven: News about regulation, adoption, technology upgrades, or major institutions entering the space can move prices sharply.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Popular Crypto Instruments
docs.content: Bitcoin (BTC/USD): Often viewed as “digital gold” by some investors. Ethereum (ETH/USD): The leading smart-contract platform token.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Risks to Understand
docs.content: Sharp price swings can lead to rapid gains or losses. Regulatory environments differ by country and can evolve. Liquidity varies between different coins—major ones are more liquid than smaller altcoins.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: 4. Commodities: Energy, Agriculture, and Raw Materials
docs.content: Commodities include tangible goods such as crude oil, natural gas, wheat, corn, and metals used in industry. They are essential to the global economy and are heavily influenced by physical supply and demand.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Types of Commodities
docs.content: Energy: Crude oil, Brent oil, natural gas Agricultural: Wheat, corn, soybeans, coffee Industrial metals: Copper, aluminum, nickel (not precious metals like gold/silver)

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: What Drives Commodity Prices
docs.content: Supply disruptions: Conflicts, sanctions, labor strikes, or production cuts can affect supply. Weather and seasons: Agricultural commodities are sensitive to droughts, floods, and seasonal patterns. Global economic activity: Strong growth can increase demand for energy and industrial metals; slowdowns can reduce it.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Trading Commodities via CFDs
docs.content: Many traders use CFDs to speculate on commodity price movements. This allows: Trading both rising and falling prices Access without handling physical goods Use of leverage (again, increasing both risk and potential reward)

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Choosing Which Market to Start With
docs.content: Each market has its pros and cons. Here are a few guiding questions: Time availability: If you want flexibility and near-constant access, forex or crypto might suit you. Risk tolerance: If you want to avoid extreme swings, you might focus on major forex pairs or gold. If you are comfortable with high volatility, crypto could be an option—provided you manage risk carefully. Interest & knowledge: If you follow central bank news, forex may fit. If you track inflation, risk sentiment, and macro headlines, gold and metals might appeal. If you are interested in energy markets or agriculture, commodities could be your focus. You don’t need to trade everything at once. Many traders start with one primary market, build experience, and slowly explore others.

docs.title: Beginner’s Guide to Global Markets: Forex, Metals, Crypto, and Commodities
docs.heading: Final Thoughts
docs.content: Forex, metals, crypto, and commodities all offer unique opportunities—but they also come with their own risks and learning curves. Before committing real capital: Spend time understanding how each market behaves Practice on a demo account Develop a risk management plan Trade sizes that you are comfortable with The goal is not to trade every market, but to find the one that fits your strategy, personality, and risk tolerance—and then grow from there with discipline and patience.

docs.title: Day Trading Indices Explained
docs.heading: Introduction
docs.content: Day trading is an active trading approach where all positions are opened and closed within the same trading day. This allows traders to take advantage of short-term price movements in global indices while avoiding overnight market risk. Successful index day trading relies on fast execution, reliable pricing, advanced charting tools, and low trading costs. While indices offer strong intraday opportunities, many traders underestimate the discipline required. Consistent results depend on proper education, a clear trading plan, effective risk management, and emotional control. Stonefort provides traders with efficient platforms and market access designed to support both short-term and strategic index trading.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: What is Forex?
docs.content: Forex, short for foreign exchange, is the global marketplace where currencies are traded. It’s the largest financial market in the world, with daily trading volumes exceeding $7 trillion. Every time you travel abroad and exchange your money for another currency, you are participating in the forex market just on a much smaller scale. Traders participate in forex to capture profit from the rise and fall of one currency against another. These movements may seem small, but with the right tools and understanding, they can create meaningful opportunities.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Why Forex Attracts Newcomers
docs.content: Forex has become one of the most accessible financial markets for beginners. Here's why:

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: High Liquidity
docs.content: The market sees massive buy and sell activity every second. This means traders can quickly enter and exit positions without large price slippage.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Market Hours
docs.content: Because forex operates across different time zones from Sydney to Tokyo, London, and New York the market runs 24 hours a day, 5 days a week. You can trade at a time that fits your schedule.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Low Starting Capital
docs.content: Many brokers allow traders to start with small amounts, making forex more accessible than stocks or commodities.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Use of Leverage
docs.content: Leverage allows traders to control larger positions with smaller capital. However, with greater opportunity comes greater risk understanding leverage is essential.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Understanding Currency Pairs
docs.content: Currencies are always traded in pairs, such as EUR/USD or GBP/JPY. The first currency is called the base currency, and the second is the quote currency. When you buy a pair, you’re buying the base currency and selling the quote currency. Example: If EUR/USD rises, it means the euro is strengthening against the US dollar.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Key Forex Terms Explained
docs.content: To trade confidently, you’ll need to understand the basic terms used across the industry.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Pip
docs.content: A pip (Percentage in Point) is the smallest unit of change in a currency pair’s price. For most pairs, one pip equals 0.0001. For JPY pairs, it’s 0.01. Pips help measure how much a trade moves and how much profit or loss it generates.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Lot
docs.content: A lot is the standardized trading size in forex. There are three main types: Standard lot: 100,000 units Mini lot: 10,000 units Micro lot: 1,000 units New traders often start with micro or mini lots to manage risk more effectively.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Spread
docs.content: The spread is the difference between the bid price (sell) and the ask price (buy). Spreads can be tight or wide depending on the market’s liquidity. Lower spreads generally benefit traders because they reduce trading costs.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Leverage
docs.content: Leverage lets traders control positions that are larger than their account balance. For example, with 1:100 leverage, $100 allows you to control $10,000 worth of currency. Leverage magnifies both earnings and losses treat it with respect.

docs.title: Forex Basics for Newcomers
docs.heading: Final Thoughts
docs.content: Forex offers a world of opportunity, but success begins with a strong foundation. Understanding terms like pips, lots, spreads, and leverage will help you approach the market with clarity and confidence. As you continue learning, practice on a demo account, study price movements, and always manage your risk wisely.

docs.title: How Do Dividends Affect Index CFDs?
docs.heading: Introduction
docs.content: If you hold an index CFD position overnight when a dividend adjustment is applied, the following may occur: Buy positions may receive a dividend adjustment Sell positions may incur a dividend-related charge These adjustments reflect the impact of dividends paid by the underlying index constituents and are applied automatically to open positions.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.content: Choosing the right forex broker is one of the most important decisions a new trader will make. A good broker can provide the tools, support, and trading environment needed to grow while a poor choice can lead to unnecessary risks and frustration. With hundreds of brokers available, it can be difficult to know where to begin. Below are the essential factors every beginner should consider.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Regulation and Safety
docs.content: Regulation should always be your first checkpoint. A regulated broker is monitored by a financial authority, which helps protect your funds and ensures fair trading practices. Look for brokers licensed by authorities such as: FCA – Financial Conduct Authority (UK) ASIC – Australian Securities and Investments Commission NFA/CFTC – United States CySEC – Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission A regulated broker must follow strict rules including segregating client funds, providing transparent pricing, and offering dispute resolution. This reduces the risk of fraud and ensures your money is handled responsibly.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Spreads, Commissions, and Fees
docs.content: Trading costs vary from broker to broker. Even small differences in spreads can have a noticeable impact on your long-term profits.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: What to compare:
docs.content: Spreads: Tight spreads mean you pay less per trade. Commissions: Some brokers charge a fixed commission per lot; others are commission-free but offer wider spreads. Swap rates: Overnight financing fees for holding positions. Hidden charges: Inactivity fees, withdrawal fees, or account maintenance costs. Always choose a broker that is transparent about pricing and doesn’t surprise you with unexpected charges.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Trading Platform and Tools
docs.content: Your trading platform is your main workspace. It should be reliable, fast, and user-friendly. Beginners generally perform best with a platform that is easy to navigate and offers helpful tools. Key features to look for: Clean and intuitive interface Fast execution speed Mobile and desktop compatibility Built-in indicators and charting tools Access to educational resources Risk-management tools like stop-loss and take-profit Popular platforms like MetaTrader 4 (MT4) and MetaTrader 5 (MT5) remain the industry standard for good reason they’re stable, flexible, and easy to learn.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Customer Support
docs.content: Quality customer support is often overlooked, but it plays a huge role in your trading experience. If you ever face technical issues, withdrawal delays, or account questions, quick support can save you from costly mistakes. Choose a broker that offers: 24/5 or 24/7 support Live chat for quick answers Multilingual assistance Helpful and knowledgeable staff For beginners, prompt support can make the learning journey much smoother.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Additional Factors to Consider
docs.content: Here are a few more details that can help you make a final decision:

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Deposit & Withdrawal Methods
docs.content: Check whether the broker offers convenient funding options such as bank transfers, cards, or e-wallets. Withdrawals should be quick and hassle-free.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Account Types
docs.content: Different account types (standard, raw, micro) support different trading styles. Pick one that suits your capital and strategy.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Educational Resources
docs.content: Some brokers offer webinars, guides, video lessons, and market analysis highly beneficial for newcomers.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Execution Quality
docs.content: Slippage, requotes, and slow execution can affect your results. A good broker executes trades smoothly, even during volatile conditions.

docs.title: How to Choose the Right Forex Broker
docs.heading: Final Thoughts
docs.content: A reliable forex broker is more than just a platform it’s a trading partner. By evaluating regulation, trading costs, platform quality, and customer support, you can choose a broker that fits your needs and protects your trading future. Take your time, compare multiple options, and always do your own research before opening an account. Smart broker selection is the first step toward long-term success in the forex market.

docs.title: Indicative Rates and Trade Adjustments
docs.heading: Introduction
docs.content: All rates shown are indicative and may change based on market conditions. Final pricing is applied at the server rollover time. Any profits or losses from index CFD trades will be reflected in your account balance with the relevant trade comments. Traders are encouraged to regularly monitor open positions and stay informed about market updates that may affect their trades.

docs.heading: 1. Forex: Currency Trading
docs.content: Forex is the global market for exchanging one currency for another. It operates 24 hours a day, 5 days a week and is the most liquid market in the world.

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: Why Traders Like Forex
docs.content: High liquidity Low spreads on major pairs Flexible trading hours Opportunity to trade rising and falling markets Common pairs include EUR/USD, GBP/USD, and USD/JPY.

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: 2. Metals: Gold & Silver

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: 3. Crypto: Digital Assets

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: 4. Commodities: Energy & Agriculture

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: What Affects Commodity Prices?

docs.title: A Quick Introduction to Forex, Metals, Crypto & Commodities
docs.heading: Final Thoughts
docs.content: Forex, metals, crypto, and commodities each offer unique opportunities. The best market for you depends on your risk tolerance, time availability, and interest. Start simple, learn the basics, and always trade with a plan.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.content: Leverage and margin are two of the most important concepts in forex trading. They give traders the ability to open larger positions than their account balance would normally allow, but they must be used with care. To trade safely and effectively, it’s essential to understand how these tools work and how they influence your overall risk.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: What Is Forex?
docs.content: Forex (short for foreign exchange) is the global market where currencies are traded. It allows traders to buy one currency while selling another, aiming to profit from changes in exchange rates.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: What Is Leverage?
docs.content: Leverage is essentially borrowed capital provided by your broker. It allows you to control a position that is larger than your actual investment. For example: With 100:1 leverage, a trader can open a $100,000 position using just $1,000 of their own funds. Leverage multiplies your buying power and as a result, both potential profits and potential losses. Even a small price movement can lead to a significant change in your account value when leverage is involved.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: What Is Margin?
docs.content: Margin is the amount of money you must deposit to open a leveraged trade. It acts as collateral and ensures you have enough capital to support your positions. Margin is not a cost or a fee it’s simply a portion of your account balance set aside while your trade is active. Example: If a broker requires 1% margin, you would need $1,000 to open a $100,000 trade. If the market moves against you, your available margin decreases. When it reaches a critical level, you may face a margin call, or the broker might automatically close your positions to prevent your balance from going negative.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: How Leverage and Margin Work Together
docs.content: Leverage determines the size of your position, while margin determines the amount of capital required to hold that position. In simple terms: Leverage = expands buying power Margin = portion of funds locked as security A clear understanding of both helps traders avoid opening positions that are too large and too risky for their account.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: Risks of High Leverage
docs.content: High leverage is appealing because it can magnify gains but it can also magnify losses with equal speed.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: 1. Faster Losses
docs.content: A small unfavorable price movement can wipe out a large portion of your account when using high leverage.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: 2. Margin Calls
docs.content: If your equity drops too low, the broker may ask for additional funds or automatically close your trades.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: 3. Emotional Trading
docs.content: Large swings in profit and loss can trigger emotional decisions, leading to inconsistent and risky behaviors.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: 4. Overconfidence
docs.content: Many beginners mistake high leverage for a shortcut to quick profits. In reality, high leverage requires even more discipline.

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: Best Practices for Using Leverage Wisely
docs.content: To protect your capital and trade responsibly: Start with lower leverage (e.g., 10:1 or 20:1) until you gain more experience Use stop-loss orders to limit potential losses Keep your position size small relative to your account Avoid trading during high-impact news unless you’re prepared for volatility Monitor your available margin at all times

docs.title: Understanding Forex Leverage and Margin
docs.heading: Final Thoughts
docs.content: Leverage and margin are powerful tools, but they can only benefit you if you understand how they work. By learning the relationship between leverage, margin, and risk, you can build a healthier trading approach and protect your long-term growth as a trader. Forex can be rewarding, but a smart trader always treats leverage with caution not excitement.

docs.title: Cryptocurrency Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Managing Crypto Trading Risk
docs.content: Cryptocurrency CFDs are typically more volatile than traditional currency markets and can experience sharp, sudden price movements driven by rapidly changing sentiment. Due to this heightened volatility, leverage available on crypto CFDs is generally lower than that offered on other asset classes. However, it remains possible to incur substantial losses within a short period if the market moves against your position. During fast-moving or uncertain market conditions, underlying spreads may also widen, which can increase trading costs or affect execution. It is important to fully understand how CFDs and cryptocurrency markets operate before trading. Consider using available risk-management tools to help manage exposure, while keeping in mind that these tools are not guaranteed and may execute at a different level if the market moves quickly or gaps.

docs.title: Cryptocurrency Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Is Cryptocurrency CFD Trading?
docs.content: Cryptocurrency CFD trading enables you to speculate on the price movements of digital assets such as Bitcoin or Ethereum without owning the underlying coins. You trade based on market direction, taking either long (buy) or short (sell) positions depending on your outlook.

docs.title: Cryptocurrency Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: How Does CFD Trading Differ From Buying Cryptocurrency Directly?
docs.content: When you purchase cryptocurrency outright, you own the asset and must store it in a digital wallet, which requires safeguarding private keys and managing transfers. With CFDs, no ownership is involved. Instead, you enter a contract to exchange the difference in price between when a position is opened and closed. CFDs are typically traded on margin, allowing you to open positions using a portion of the total value. Margin trading increases both potential gains and potential losses. CFDs also allow you to take short positions if you anticipate a decline in the asset’s price.

docs.title: Cryptocurrency Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Do I Need a Crypto Wallet to Trade CFDs?
docs.content: No. Because CFD trading does not involve buying the underlying cryptocurrency, there is no requirement for a digital wallet.

docs.heading: What Influences Cryptocurrency Prices?
docs.content: Cryptocurrency prices can be affected by multiple factors, including: Supply dynamics: Assets like Bitcoin have fixed maximum supply limits. Issuance or mining rates: Events such as halvings can influence market expectations. Market sentiment: News, industry developments, social media activity and broader public interest can drive volatility. Regulatory changes: Adjustments in government policy or taxation frameworks may impact demand. Economic conditions: Inflation, interest rates and fiat currency strength can influence crypto flows. Technological developments: Advancements or setbacks in blockchain applications may affect appeal. Institutional adoption: Involvement from banks, funds or corporations can shift market liquidity. Exchange liquidity: Lower-liquidity venues can experience sharper price fluctuations.

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What is Forex?
docs.content: Forex, also known as foreign exchange or FX, refers to the global marketplace where currencies are traded. It is a decentralized market that operates 24 hours a day, five days a week, and is considered the largest and most liquid financial market in the world. Forex trading involves exchanging one currency for another with the intention of benefiting from fluctuations in their exchange rates.

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: How Do I Trade Forex?
docs.content: Forex trading is the process of speculating on the movement of currency prices. When you trade forex, you are essentially predicting whether one currency will strengthen or weaken against another. Example: If you believe the Euro will increase in value relative to the US Dollar, you buy the currency pair EUR/USD. If you expect the Euro to weaken, you sell EUR/USD instead. Your profit or loss depends on how the market moves after you enter the trade.

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Are Some Forex Trading Examples?
docs.content: Understanding real market scenarios makes forex easier to grasp. Here are simple illustrations:

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Example 1: Buying (Going Long)
docs.content: You expect GBP to strengthen against USD. Price moves from 1.2500 to 1.2550 after your buy order. That 50-pip rise becomes your profit depending on your lot size.

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Example 2: Selling (Going Short)
docs.content: You expect USD to weaken against JPY. You sell USD/JPY at 150.00 and close at 149.60. The 40-pip drop benefits your short position. These examples show how traders take advantage of price movements in either direction.

docs.title: Forex Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Is Margin in Forex?
docs.content: Margin in forex is the amount of money a trader must set aside in their trading account to open and maintain a leveraged position. Think of it as a security deposit held by the broker while your trade is active. It does not represent the cost of the trade itself, but rather the portion of your capital required to support the position. Margin allows you to control a much larger position size than the amount you physically deposit, making it a key component of leveraged trading. While this increases your profit potential, it also increases your exposure to losses and must be managed with caution.

docs.heading: What Are the Benefits of Trading CFDs on Indices?
docs.content: Trading CFDs on indices allows you to gain exposure to an entire market or economic sector through a single instrument. Instead of trading individual stocks, you can trade the overall performance of major indices such as the S&P 500, NASDAQ, or FTSE, benefiting from broader market trends. Index CFDs also require less capital compared to instruments such as ETFs or futures, as leverage enables traders to control larger positions with a smaller initial investment. This makes index trading more capital-efficient for active traders. In addition, index CFDs closely track the spot price of the underlying index. Unlike futures or options, they are not affected by expiration dates or time decay, allowing traders to focus purely on price movement and market direction.

docs.title: Indices Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Factors Influence the Price of Indices?
docs.content: Index prices are primarily influenced by the performance of the individual companies that make up the index. Strong or weak earnings, sector growth, and changes in business outlook can directly impact index movements. Broader macroeconomic factors also play a significant role. Interest rate decisions by central banks often influence indices, as changes in borrowing costs affect corporate profits and investor sentiment. Inflation data, employment figures, and economic growth indicators can further drive market direction. Geopolitical developments such as elections, policy changes, trade agreements, or global conflicts can create volatility in index markets. Government actions, including taxation policies, regulations, and fiscal stimulus, may also support or pressure index performance.

docs.heading: Are Stonefort Index CFDs Based on Spot or Futures Prices?

docs.title: Markets
docs.heading: What is Forex?
docs.content: Forex, or foreign exchange, is the global marketplace where currencies are traded. Operating 24 hours a day, it is the largest and most liquid financial market in the world. Traders buy and sell currency pairs with the aim of profiting from changes in exchange rates.

docs.title: Markets
docs.heading: How do I trade Forex?
docs.content: Forex trading involves speculating on whether one currency will strengthen or weaken against another. You trade currency pairs, such as EUR/USD, by deciding if the base currency will rise or fall in value. If you expect the Euro to gain against the US Dollar, you would buy EUR/USD; if you expect it to fall, you would sell it.

docs.title: Markets
docs.heading: What is margin in Forex?
docs.content: Margin is the initial deposit required to open a leveraged forex position. It allows you to control a larger trade size with a smaller amount of capital, increasing both your potential profits and potential losses.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Are Commodities?
docs.content: Commodities are raw materials that support global production, transportation and manufacturing. They include metals such as gold, silver, copper and nickel, energy products like oil and natural gas, and agricultural goods such as wheat, cotton, coffee, cocoa and livestock. These assets form the foundation of many industries and play a significant role in shaping economic activity worldwide.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Why Do People Trade Commodities?
docs.content: Commodity trading originally developed as a way for producers and consumers to manage price risk on the goods they depended on. Over time, the market has expanded to include individual and institutional traders who take positions based on expectations of future price movements. Traders may buy or sell commodities depending on whether they anticipate prices to rise or fall, using these markets to diversify their portfolios or respond to global economic events.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Affects Commodity Prices?
docs.content: Commodity prices are influenced by a wide range of factors because of their close connection to global economic and geopolitical conditions. Key influences include: Supply and demand changes: Poor harvests, supply shortages or production cuts can reduce availability and push prices higher. Geopolitical developments: Conflicts or trade disruptions can affect supply chains, especially for energy and metals. Industry demand: Growing demand in sectors like technology, construction or manufacturing can increase the need for certain raw materials. Economic uncertainty: During periods of market stress, investors often turn to assets such as gold, which is traditionally viewed as a store of value.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: What Is a Forward Contract?
docs.content: A forward contract is a privately negotiated agreement between two parties to buy or sell an asset at a specific price on a future date. Because forwards are traded over the counter rather than on an exchange, they can be tailored to suit the needs of the participants. This includes flexibility in asset type, contract size, settlement terms and expiry dates. Unlike standardized futures, forwards do not trade on a centralised exchange, allowing for greater customisation but also introducing counterparty risk, since settlement depends on the parties involved.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Forwards
docs.content: Traded over the counter (OTC) directly between two parties. Fully customisable, with terms such as quantity, price and settlement date agreed privately. Not standardised and not cleared through an exchange.

docs.title: Commodities & Metals Trading with Stonefort
docs.heading: Futures
docs.content: Traded on organised exchanges like CME or Eurex. Standardised contract terms defined by the exchange. Supported by clearing houses, which help reduce counterparty risk.

docs.title: Stock Trading  with Stonefort
docs.heading: How Does Stock CFD Trading Work with Stonefort?
docs.content: Stock trading through CFDs allows you to speculate on the price movement of company shares without owning the underlying stock. Instead of purchasing physical shares, you trade contracts based on whether a stock’s price is expected to rise or fall, offering greater flexibility and efficiency. One of the key advantages of trading stock CFDs with Stonefort is access to global equity markets from a single platform. With a relatively small initial deposit, traders can gain exposure to leading international stocks and take advantage of both upward and downward market movements. Stonefort offers leverage of up to 1:20 on stock CFDs, enabling traders to increase their market exposure with lower capital outlay. For example, a $1,000 margin can provide exposure equivalent to $20,000 in stock value. While leverage can magnify potential returns, it also increases risk — making sound risk management

docs.heading: How Is Profit Calculated?
docs.content: Opening Price 40 shares × $180 = $7,200 Closing Price 40 shares × $195 = $7,800 Total Profit from Trading $7,800 − $7,200 = $600

docs.title: Stock Trading  with Stonefort
docs.heading: The Position Opened
docs.content: 40 CFDs of a stock were traded at an opening price of $180 per share. The total value of the position is: 40 × $180 = $7,200 With a 10% margin requirement, the required trading capital is: 10% of $7,200 = $720 This allows you to control a larger position with a smaller initial deposit.

docs.title: Stock Trading  with Stonefort
docs.heading: Position Closure
docs.content: Two weeks later, the stock price rises to $195 per share. You close your position at this higher price. Total closing value: 40 × $195 = $7,800 Total profit realized from the price movement is: $600
